name: Practical Grotesk
classification_notes: The name and specimen text referencing Swiss place names point squarely to the grotesque tradition. The wide weight range from Hairline to Black with matching italics is consistent with a workhorse grotesque family.
summary: A workhorse grotesque spanning ten weights from Hairline to Black, each with a matching italic, suited to editorial and identity work.
description: Twenty styles cover a broad weight range from Hairline through to Black, with italic companions throughout. The specimen text draws on Swiss place names and French-language settings, suggesting the typeface is at home in multilingual European contexts. Its straightforward grotesque construction keeps it practical across print and screen without asserting a strong personality of its own.
foundry_blurb: Apex Foundry present Practical Grotesk as a grotesque typeface available in twenty styles, running from Hairline Italic to Black Italic, with OpenType feature support across the full range.

name: Nicéphore
classification_notes: The typeface exhibits humanist sans-serif characteristics, with alternates for letters such as G, R, Q, a, g, t and y suggesting warmth and calligraphic influence typical of the humanist tradition.
summary: A six-weight humanist sans named after Nicéphore Niépce, the French inventor credited with creating the world's first permanent photographic image.
description: Named in tribute to Joseph Nicéphore Niépce, the typeface runs from Thin to Heavy across six weights with no italics. It carries an extensive Latin character set covering a wide range of European languages, backed by a thorough OpenType feature set including oldstyle figures, tabular variants, historical ligatures, stylistic alternates for several key letters and a full suite of mathematical and geometric symbols. The alternates for characters such as G, R, Q, t and y give designers flexibility to adjust the voice of the text without switching families.
foundry_blurb: Apex Foundry present Nicéphore as a six-weight sans-serif with an extensive OpenType feature set including multiple figure styles, stylistic alternates, ligatures and broad Latin language support. The name references Nicéphore Niépce, the Burgundian inventor who pioneered heliography and created some of the earliest surviving photographic images in the 1820s.

name: Rifugio
classification_notes: Rifugio presents clean, upright letterforms with no italic cut and a wide range of weights from Thin to Black, consistent with a geometric or neo-grotesque sans-serif tradition. The absence of condensed variants and the structured alternate characters suggest a refined geometric approach.
summary: A geometric sans-serif from Apex Foundry spanning eight weights, named after an Italian mountain refuge at the foot of Mont Blanc.
description: Eight weights from Thin to Black give the family considerable range for both text and display use. The glyph set is extensive, with small capitals, old-style figures, tabular variants, alternates for a, g, r, t and y, and a wide spread of OpenType features including fractions, superiors and inferiors. Latin Extended coverage reaches a broad range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: Apex Foundry's Rifugio takes its name from the Rifugio Torino, an Italian mountain hut situated at 3,375 metres in the Mont Blanc massif near the Pointe Helbronner. The family ships in eight weights with a rich OpenType feature set including small capitals, multiple numeral styles, alternates and an extensive Latin Extended character set.

name: Kellar
classification_notes: Kellar draws directly from nineteenth-century Condensed Titles cuts in the MS&J tradition, with the high contrast, narrow proportions and Didot-like fashion characteristics that place it firmly in the condensed didone display category.
summary: A condensed didone display typeface synthesised from nineteenth-century MS&J Condensed Titles cuts, with organic lowercases, expressive figures and a pronounced oval structure.
description: Named after Thomas MacKellar of the MacKellar, Smiths and Jordan foundry, Kellar is built from a study of MS&J's Printer's Handy Book of Specimens (1871). High contrast and narrow proportions follow Didot-influenced fashion typefaces of the period, but the organic lowercases and expressive figures give it a distinct, eccentric character. Available in Regular and Italic, both carrying a comprehensive OpenType feature set including small caps, multiple figure sets and a generous range of alternates.
foundry_blurb: Kellar was named after Thomas MacKellar, associate owner of MacKellar, Smiths and Jordan, one of the leading American type foundries of the nineteenth century. The typeface began as a synthesis of various Condensed Titles cuts from MS&J's Printer's Handy Book of Specimens (1871), incorporating the high contrast and narrow proportions typical of Didot-like fashion typefaces. The result is described as a quirky outsider, distinguished by organic lowercases, expressive figures and an emphasised oval structure.

name: Marsel
classification_notes: Marsel is a sans-serif with variable contrast across weights, shifting from a near-monoline construction in lighter cuts to a high-contrast, personality-driven form in heavier weights, drawing on humanist influences from Excoffon's Antique Olive and Gill's experimental work.
summary: A sans-serif family conceived as a meeting between Roger Excoffon and Eric Gill, shifting from near-monoline in lighter weights to high-contrast exuberance in the Black.
description: Beginning in 2016 as a single Black cut inspired by Excoffon's Antique Olive Nord and Gill's Kayo, Marsel expanded into a seven-weight family where the distribution of thin and thick strokes grows increasingly eccentric as weight increases. The result is a family with a split character: restrained and rational at the lighter end, boldly contrasted and expressive at the heavy end. It ships as a variable font with a thorough OpenType feature set including alternates for a, g, y and Q, four figure styles and extensive Latin language coverage.
foundry_blurb: Apex Foundry describe Marsel as the imagined result of Roger Excoffon and Eric Gill meeting in a Marseille backyard over Pastis. Originally a single Black display cut homaging Antique Olive Nord, it grew from 2019 into a full family spanning Thin to Black, with the defining characteristic being a shift in stroke contrast across the weight range — from monoline discretion to Provençal exuberance. Now available as a variable font with contemporary OpenType features, ligatures, multiple figure styles and alternative letterforms.

name: Granit Display
classification_notes: Granit Display is a display serif drawing on vernacular letterforms from 19th and 20th century headstones, giving it a distinctive lapidary character that sits firmly in the display category rather than any conventional serif subcategory.
summary: A display serif by Alex Chavot drawn from vernacular letterforms found on 19th and 20th century headstones, including those at Père-Lachaise cemetery.
description: Alex Chavot gathered letterforms from funerary stonecutting across two centuries and reworked them into a display typeface with a contemporary sensibility. The family ships in Regular and Italic, with an extensive OpenType feature set including small capitals, discretionary ligatures, multiple numeral styles and stylistic alternates for r and y. The depth of the glyph set and the historical grounding of the letterforms make it well suited to editorial and cultural work where a singular, material quality is required.
foundry_blurb: Apex Foundry describe Granit Display as a typeface gleaned from the vernacular letterforms of 19th and 20th century headstones, with Père-Lachaise cemetery among the noted sources of inspiration. Designer Alex Chavot revisits these varied sources into a display typeface with a modern sensibility, opening with a quote from Yvon Chouinard on the living quality of granite itself.

name: Almeria
classification_notes: Almeria is described as a bold and distinctive serif that shuns historical models or direct revivals, suggesting an original display serif design with high contrast inspired by Andalusian visual culture rather than any classical tradition.
summary: An original display serif from Apex Foundry, drawing on the arid landscapes of Southern Spain rather than any historical typeface tradition.
description: Almeria is a bold, high-contrast serif built without reference to historical models, its character shaped instead by the stark visual contrasts of Andalusia. The family ships in Regular and Italic, with an extensive Latin character set covering small capitals, old-style figures, a wide range of ligatures and a substantial suite of OpenType features including circled figures and alternative arrows. The specimen positions it around the literature of Don Quixote, suggesting an affinity for literary and editorial settings with a strong voice.
foundry_blurb: Apex Foundry describe Almeria as a bold and distinctive serif family that shuns historical models or direct revivals, born almost entirely from its designer's own imagination. The typeface takes its name and character from the arid, sun-drenched landscapes of Southern Spain, with its design echoing the striking contrasts of Andalusian architecture and light.

name: Smithee
classification_notes: Derived from a vintage wood type specimen, Smithee is an extra-condensed display sans with mechanical structure and organic quirks, suited to high-impact titling rather than text setting.
summary: An extra-condensed display sans derived from a vintage French wood type specimen, available as a variable font across five weights.
description: Drawn from a 'Série A' wood type specimen sourced from the Fonderie Typographique Française archives in Lyon, Smithee balances mechanical symmetry in letters like b, d, p and q against idiosyncratic forms in a and s. Its silhouette recalls both Edward Wright's titling for the 1956 'This Is Tomorrow' exhibition and the billing-block credits at the foot of film posters, which inspired its name after the famous directorial pseudonym Alan Smithee. Offered as a variable font with five weight stops, it carries a rich OpenType feature set including six stylistic sets, old-style figures and extensive Latin language support.
foundry_blurb: Apex Foundry describe Smithee as rooted in a vintage wood type specimen from the Fonderie Typographique Française, discovered in the archives of Lyon's Musée de l'imprimerie. The design focuses on the most condensed style in the original range, noting the tension between its mechanical structure and organic quirks. The name references Alan Smithee, the pseudonym used by film directors who disowned a film after it was recut against their wishes. Now a variable font, Smithee spans five weights and is pitched as a daring tool for expressive, high-impact typography.

name: Pyros
classification_notes: The glyph set and specimen text suggest a text-oriented serif with classical proportions; the inclusion of small capitals, old-style figures and extensive OpenType features points to a transitional or humanist serif tradition.
summary: A text serif from Apex Foundry offering two styles with an extensive OpenType feature set including small capitals, multiple figure sets and circled numerals.
description: Available in Regular and Italic, Pyros is a Latin-script text serif with broad language coverage across well over a hundred languages. Its OpenType palette includes lining, old-style, tabular and tabular old-style figures, small capitals, superior and inferior letters, fractions, roman numerals, and stylistic sets for circled figures and alternative arrows. The specimen leans on Werner Herzog's documentary writing about volcanoes, lending the typeface a literary, essayistic tone.
foundry_blurb: Apex Foundry present Pyros as a feature-rich text typeface with Regular and Italic styles, showcasing its extensive glyph set through specimen text drawn from Werner Herzog's writings on volcanoes and documentary filmmaking. OpenType features include small capitals, multiple numeral styles, discretionary ligatures, circled figures and alternative arrows.

name: Hazel Display
classification_notes: Hazel Display is positioned explicitly as a display typeface, with its name and specimen copy evoking gothic and vampire aesthetics, suggesting high-contrast or decorative letterforms suited to large-scale headline use rather than text setting.
summary: A two-style display typeface from Apex Foundry with gothic atmospheric leanings, extensive OpenType feature support and broad Latin language coverage.
description: Available in Regular and Italic, Hazel Display suits headline and title work where a dark, literary mood is wanted. The font ships with a thorough OpenType feature set including small capitals, old-style and tabular figures, circled figure variants, discretionary ligatures, superior and inferior figures, and alternative arrows. Latin language coverage is wide, spanning several hundred languages across Extended Latin.
foundry_blurb: Apex Foundry present Hazel Display as a typeface with a gothic and nocturnal character, demonstrated through specimen text drawn from vampire literature and cinema. The family includes Regular and Italic styles with a comprehensive glyph set covering multiple figure styles, small capitals, ligatures, geometric symbols and miscellaneous icons.
